items.q: Does Kc change when more reactant is added at constant temperature?
items.correct: No — Kc is fixed at a given temperature; the concentrations adjust until the same equilibrium ratio is restored.
items.misconception: Adding reactant increases Kc because more product is formed.
items.random_wrong: Adding reactant always halves the value of Kc.
items.why: Students confuse the position of equilibrium, which shifts, with the equilibrium constant, which only temperature can change.

items.q: At equilibrium, have the forward and reverse reactions stopped?
items.correct: No — both continue at equal rates, so concentrations stay constant; the equilibrium is dynamic.
items.misconception: At equilibrium the reaction has finished and nothing is happening.
items.random_wrong: At equilibrium only the reverse reaction keeps going.
items.why: Constant macroscopic concentrations look static, so students miss the ongoing molecular-level exchange in both directions.

items.q: Are mean bond enthalpies exact for every molecule?
items.correct: No — they are averages over many compounds, so ΔH values calculated from them are only approximate.
items.misconception: Bond enthalpy calculations give the exact ΔH for any reaction.
items.random_wrong: Bond enthalpies apply only to ionic compounds.
items.why: Students treat tabulated values as universal constants, not averages; the same bond differs in strength between molecules.

items.q: In pV = nRT with p in Pa and V in m³, the gas constant R is:
items.why: Correct answer: 8.31 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. “96 500 C mol⁻¹” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Breaking a chemical bond is always:
items.why: Correct answer: Endothermic. “Exothermic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which change alters the value of Kc?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Changing the temperature. “Adding more reactant” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Increasing the pressure on N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ shifts the equilibrium:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Right, towards fewer gas moles. “Left, towards more gas moles” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many moles of gas are in 4.8 dm³ at room temperature and pressure (1 mol occupies 24 dm³)?
items.why: Exact answer: 0.2 mol. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
